So Bali Wine dates back to the mid-19th century when Bass or Bass Rat Cliff and Gretton, as they were called at the time, based in Burton on Trent, here in the UK, they began a production of a single brew Bali Wine that they simply called number one, that was the name of it, Bass number one. And the label was adorned with a single red diamond, and this single red diamond was actually Britain's second registered trademark ever, which I didn't know and I find that quite interesting. The first one was Bass's red triangle that you can still see on Bass beers even today. Number one, as it is still known, was brewed almost continuously from 1854 through to 1995 when it was discontinued. There was a 10 year break between 1944 and 1954, but even though it is no longer being made, it still is the gold standard of Bali wines, which other Bali wines are measured against, which is really intriguing because up until the time I started to put this together, they've even heard of Bass number one. But Bass number one was not distinctive only for the method of production, but also because it was so pale. The reason it was so pale is because of the maltz that it was using. Prior to this, Bali wines had mainly been like a dark amber to dark browning colour, which were made with much cheaper and darker maltz. But this pale Bali wine was quite novel at its time, but then after Bass started producing their pale Bali wine, there were some other brewers that followed suit as well with pale versions full of gold and pride, and also tenants gold label.
